sentence: Subjective:

The patient, a 45-year-old male with a history of diabetes type two and mild intermittent asthma, presents with a complaint of right elbow pain that started three weeks ago after increasing his tennis activity.
He describes the pain as sharp, stabbing, and throbbing, rating it an 8/10 with movement and 3/10 at rest.
He has tried taking Tylenol and ibuprofen, but they do not provide relief.
He also mentions that ice does not help much.
He has a history of playing tennis in the past and is a bit concerned about his current level of activity.
Past medical history:

The patient has a history of diabetes type two, for which he is currently taking metformin 500mg twice a day, and mild intermittent asthma, for which he is taking Flovent twice a day and albuterol as needed.
He has a history of penicillin allergy and had his gallbladder removed about 10 years ago.
Review of systems:
The patient denies any fever, and there are no other symptoms mentioned.
Current medications:
Metformin 500mg twice a day for diabetes type two; Flovent twice a day and albuterol as needed for asthma.
Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]

Physical exam: The patient has a moderate lateral epicondylar tenderness of the right elbow, with pain on passive range of motion of the right elbow, mild pain with resisted extension of the right wrist, and a normal unremarkable exam otherwise.
The patient has a grade three out of six systolic ejection murmur, but this has not changed from prior exams.
Test Results:
The right elbow x-ray shows no acute fracture or other bony abnormality, with no malalignment or sign of joint effusion.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has acute lateral epicondylitis of the right elbow, also known as tennis elbow, which is likely due to overuse of the elbow.
Problem list: Acute lateral epicondylitis of the right elbow (confirmed); Diabetes type two (active); Asthma (active).
Plan:
I recommended that the patient rest his right arm as much as possible, stop tennis for 4-6 weeks, and avoid repetitive stress in the right arm.
I also prescribed ultram 50mg once a day for pain relief.
I ordered a hemoglobin A1c level, a CBC blood test, and a chem-12 blood test to check the patient's diabetes control.
I also prescribed a refill of albuterol for asthma.
Follow-up:
I will see the patient in 4 weeks to reevaluate the elbow and consider additional imaging or a physical therapy referral if necessary.
